title: How TSMC Made Taiwan the World's Technology Heartland
gist: TSMC's revenue nearly doubled from NT$2.1 trillion in 2023 to NT$3.8 trillion in 2025 with net profit more than doubling, driven by two structural moats: the Open Innovation Platform that ties global chip design directly to TSMC's process infrastructure, and a supplier-elevation strategy that has converted Taiwan's domestic supply chain to semiconductor-grade standards. Taiwan has become not merely a node but the hub of global semiconductor supply.

insights.claim: TSMC's revenue climbed from NT$2.1 trillion in 2023 to NT$3.8 trillion in 2025, with net profit more than doubling over the same period — a near 81% revenue increase in two years driven primarily by AI chip demand.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: TSMC's two-year revenue trajectory is among the fastest of any large-cap semiconductor company; this pace of profit growth sets a high reinvestment baseline for capacity expansion and R&D.
insights.quote: TSMC's revenue climbed from NT$2.1 trillion in 2023 to NT$3.8 trillion in 2025, with net profit more than doubling over the same period.

insights.claim: AMD CEO Lisa Su announced AMD would be the first company to mass-produce high-speed chips on TSMC's 2-nanometer process, signaling TSMC's 2nm leadership over Samsung and Intel.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: AMD's 2nm commitment reinforces TSMC's first-mover process advantage; watch for announcements from Nvidia, Apple, and Qualcomm on 2nm timing as competitive proof points.
insights.quote: AMD would be the first company to mass-produce high-speed chips on TSMC's 2-nanometer process.

themes.theme: Open Innovation Platform as Ecosystem Lock-In

insights.claim: TSMC's Open Innovation Platform (OIP), established quietly before Morris Chang's retirement, embeds the most advanced process parameters directly into EDA design tools so that on the day a new process node is ready, customers can begin designing immediately — making TSMC's process parameters the de-facto design starting point.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: OIP creates structural switching costs for chip designers beyond just manufacturing relationships; competitors need to replicate both manufacturing capability and the full design ecosystem to truly compete with TSMC.
insights.quote: on the day a new process is ready, customers can begin designing immediately.

insights.claim: TSMC employs roughly 2,500 software engineers across Taiwan and the United States working with EDA vendors, IP providers, and customers to embed process parameters into design tools — a software layer that makes the foundry model stickier than manufacturing relationships alone.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: TSMC's 2,500-person software engineering force is an often-overlooked competitive moat; compare this investment to competing foundries' EDA co-development headcount to assess ecosystem gap.
insights.quote: TSMC employs roughly 2,500 software engineers across Taiwan and the United States, working alongside EDA software vendors, IP providers, and customers

insights.claim: Cerebras Systems' wafer-scale chip — the most unconventional chip architecture in recent memory — required years of close collaboration with TSMC to achieve mass production, illustrating that cutting-edge customers with novel architectures have no alternative to TSMC for the most demanding production challenges.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: TSMC's ability to solve novel packaging and manufacturing challenges for architectural outliers like Cerebras reinforces its role as the only viable partner for next-generation chip concepts at scale.

insights.claim: TSMC's yield focus has forced suppliers across Taiwan to adopt 'semiconductor-grade standards' in their own technologies and governance, converting companies in petrochemicals (Chang Chun, LCY Chemical), precision machining (Gallant Precision), and MEMS testing (WinWay Technology) into world-class semiconductor supply-chain participants.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Taiwan's supply chain depth and quality is structurally hard to replicate in any alternative geography; geographic diversification away from Taiwan involves rebuilding this entire supplier ecosystem, not just fab capacity.
insights.quote: TSMC's relentless focus on yield has forced its suppliers to apply 'semiconductor-grade standards' to their technologies, governance and management.

insights.claim: International equipment and materials leaders Merck, Lam Research, and Applied Materials have established research and application centers in southern Taiwan specifically to participate in TSMC process development in real time, deepening their technical dependency on the Taiwan ecosystem.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The presence of top equipment/materials suppliers' R&D centers in Taiwan creates reciprocal dependency — these companies' future roadmaps are structurally tied to TSMC's process choices, which investors should factor into equipment sector analysis.
insights.quote: Merck, Lam Research, and Applied Materials have all established research and application centers in southern Taiwan.

insights.claim: Revenue generated within the Southern Taiwan Science Park grew from approximately NT$800 billion in 2020 to nearly NT$3 trillion in 2025 — a near 4x increase — and surpassed the Hsinchu Science Park for the first time in 2023, meaning critical semiconductor capacity is increasingly concentrated in one region of one island.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The continued concentration of cutting-edge capacity in southern Taiwan is a growing geopolitical risk concentration point; any disruption event affects an increasingly large share of global advanced chip output.
insights.quote: Revenue generated within the Southern Taiwan Science Park grew from roughly NT$800 billion in 2020 to nearly NT$3 trillion in 2025

themes.theme: Trust as TSMC's Foundational Strategic Asset

insights.claim: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang cited 'intangible trust' as his reason for placing his company's chip manufacturing entirely with TSMC — suggesting TSMC's competitive position rests on something beyond process node metrics that is very difficult to transfer to competing foundries.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: TSMC's most valuable competitive asset is non-quantifiable trust built over decades; this is not replicable by capital expenditure alone, which is why Intel Foundry and Samsung Foundry struggle despite massive investment.
insights.quote: I trust them to put my company on top of them. That's a very big deal.
